name: xception

members.name: XceptionConfig
members.signature: XceptionConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, dropout: float = 0.5)
members.extended:
Replaces all Inception modules with depthwise separable convolutions.
Designed for 299Ă—299 inputs.

`dropout` â€” head dropout rate (0.5 in the paper).

members.name: Xception
members.extended:
Implements the "Extreme Inception" topology from Chollet,
"Xception: Deep Learning with Depthwise Separable Convolutions",
CVPR 2017 ([arXiv:1610.02357](https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.02357)).  The architecture replaces every
Inception module of Inception-v3 with a depthwise separable
convolution â€” the limiting case of an Inception block in which
every output channel of the pointwise convolution receives its
own independent spatial filter.  This makes the cross-channel
and spatial correlations *fully decoupled*, dramatically
reducing parameters and FLOPs:

$$
D_K^2 \cdot M \cdot N \;\longrightarrow\;
    M \cdot N \;+\; D_K^2 \cdot M.
$$

The body is organised into three phases â€” *entry flow* (3
blocks, with 1Ă—1 strided residuals and channel doubling from
64 â†’ 728), *middle flow* (8 identical 728-ch blocks with
additive identity shortcuts), and *exit flow* (1 strided
transition block plus two final separable convolutions
expanding to 1536 â†’ 2048 channels).  Designed for a 299Ă—299
input â€” the same crop size as Inception-v3 â€” so the
spatial-reduction schedule isolates the depthwise-separable
factorisation as the sole source of accuracy improvement.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use the `xception` factory
for the paper-cited configuration.

examples:
Build an Xception backbone and run a forward pass at the
native 299Ă—299 resolution:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.xception import xception
>>> backbone = xception()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(2, 2048, 1, 1)
notes:
Sub-module attribute names match timm's `legacy_xception`
layout so that state-dict round-trips are key-compatible with
standard pretrained weight files.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: bn1, bn2
attributes.description: BatchNorm layers paired with the stem convs.

attributes.name: block1, block2, block3
attributes.description:
Entry-flow blocks â€” strided 1Ă—1 residual shortcuts with
channel projections 64â†’128, 128â†’256, 256â†’728.

attributes.name: block4, â€¦, block11
attributes.description:
Eight identical middle-flow blocks with additive identity
shortcuts at 728 channels.

attributes.name: conv3, conv4
attributes.description:
Final two separable convolutions expanding to 1536 and
2048 channels.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the 2048-ch feature map to
`(B, 2048, 1, 1)`.

members.name: XceptionForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: Xception with dropout + linear classification head.
members.extended:
Combines an `Xception` backbone with a dropout layer
(probability `config.dropout`, default 0.5 per the paper) and
a linear projection to `config.num_classes` logits.  When
`labels` are supplied to `forward`, a cross-entropy
loss is computed and returned alongside the logits inside an
`XceptionOutput` dataclass.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use the `xception_cls` factory for
the paper-cited configuration.

examples:
Run inference at the native 299Ă—299 input resolution:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.xception import xception_cls
>>> model = xception_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(4, 1000)
notes:
The classification flow is



$$
\text{logits} = W \,\operatorname{Drop}\!\left(
    \operatorname{GAP}(\,\mathrm{backbone}(x)\,)
\right) + b.
$$

Chollet (2017) reports 79.0% top-1 ImageNet validation
accuracy with this configuration at the 299Ă—299 input
resolution, edging out Inception-v3's 78.2% at the same
parameter count (~22.9M).

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: conv1, conv2, bn1, bn2, block1, â€¦, block12, conv3, conv4, bn3, bn4, avgpool
attributes.description:
Same backbone components as on `Xception`; see that
class for shape semantics.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Built by `ClassificationHeadMixin._build_classifier` â€”
a `lucid.nn.Sequential` wrapping
`lucid.nn.Dropout` and `lucid.nn.Linear`.

members.name: XceptionOutput
members.signature: XceptionOutput(logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None)
members.extended:
Carries the per-class logits and the optional supervised loss
computed when training labels are supplied to `forward`.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.xception import xception_cls
>>> model = xception_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 1000)
>>> out.loss is None
True
notes:
Returned by `XceptionForImageClassification.forward` so the
public API stays decoupled from raw tensor tuples â€” callers can
address fields by name (`out.logits`) and ignore irrelevant
entries.

attributes.name: logits
attributes.description:
Pre-softmax classification scores of shape
`(batch_size, num_classes)`.  Apply `lucid.softmax` or
`lucid.argmax` to convert to probabilities or hard
predictions.

attributes.name: loss
attributes.description:
Scalar cross-entropy loss; present only when `labels` were
passed to the forward call.  `None` during inference.

members.name: xception
members.extended:
Builds an `Xception` with the paper-cited "Extreme
Inception" topology from Chollet, 2017: a 2-conv stem, three
entry-flow blocks (1Ă—1 strided residuals with channel
progression 64 â†’ 128 â†’ 256 â†’ 728), eight middle-flow blocks at
728 channels, one exit-flow transition block (728 â†’ 1024),
and two final separable convolutions expanding to 1536 â†’ 2048
channels.  Approximately 22.9M parameters â€” the same budget as
Inception-v3 but with depthwise separable convolutions
throughout.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored â€” the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `XceptionConfig`
(e.g. `in_channels=1` for grayscale input).

returns.description:
Backbone with the canonical Xception configuration applied
(or with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.xception import xception
>>> model = xception()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 2048, 1, 1)
notes:
See Chollet, "Xception: Deep Learning with Depthwise Separable
Convolutions", CVPR 2017 ([arXiv:1610.02357](https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.02357)).  Designed for
299Ă—299 input â€” the same crop as Inception-v3 â€” so the
spatial-reduction schedule isolates the depthwise-separable
factorisation as the sole source of accuracy improvement.

members.name: xception_cls
members.signature: xception_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: XceptionWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Xception image classifier (backbone + dropout + linear head).
members.extended:
Builds an `XceptionForImageClassification` with the
canonical Xception backbone (Chollet, 2017) followed by
dropout ($p = 0.5$) and a linear projection to
`config.num_classes` (default 1000 for ImageNet-1k).
Approximately 22.9M parameters and 79.0% ImageNet-1k top-1
accuracy at 299Ă—299 (paper, Table 5) â€” outperforming
Inception-v3 at the same parameter budget.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` â†’ random init; `True`
â†’ the `DEFAULT` tag (`XceptionWeights.TF_IN1K`); a tag
string (e.g. `"TF_IN1K"`) â†’ that specific checkpoint.
Mutually exclusive with `weights` (which wins if both are
given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member, e.g.
`XceptionWeights.TF_IN1K`.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `XceptionConfig`
(typically `num_classes` to retarget the classifier).

returns.description:
Classifier with the canonical Xception configuration
applied (or with `overrides` merged on top of it),
optionally initialised from pretrained weights.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.xception import xception_cls
>>> model = xception_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 10)
Load ImageNet-pretrained weights:
>>> model = xception_cls(pretrained=True)            # DEFAULT tag
>>> from lucid.models.vision.xception import XceptionWeights
>>> model = xception_cls(weights=XceptionWeights.TF_IN1K)
notes:
See Chollet, "Xception: Deep Learning with Depthwise Separable
Convolutions", CVPR 2017 ([arXiv:1610.02357](https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.02357)), Table 5.  Pretrained
weights are converted from timm's `legacy_xception.tf_in1k` and
hosted on the Hugging Face Hub under `lucid-dl/xception`.  The
eval preset is 299 crop / 333 resize / bicubic / mean=std=0.5 (not
ImageNet stats).

members.name: XceptionWeights
members.extended:
Ships a single ImageNet-1k checkpoint (`TF_IN1K`) converted
from timm's `legacy_xception.tf_in1k`, re-hosted under
`huggingface.co/lucid-dl/xception`.

citation: Chollet, FranĂ§ois. "Xception: Deep Learning with Depthwise Separable Convolutions." Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2017, pp. 1251â€“1258.
theory:
Xception ("Extreme Inception") interprets the Inception module
as an *intermediate* point on a continuum between an ordinary
convolution and a fully decoupled spatial/channel filter.  A
standard Inception block splits the input into a few parallel
branches, each of which is processed by a small $1\times1$
pointwise convolution followed by a spatial convolution, and the
branch outputs are concatenated.  Xception pushes this idea to
its limit: instead of a handful of branches, *every output
channel* of the $1\times1$ pointwise convolution gets its
own independent $3\times3$ spatial filter.  This is exactly
the **depthwise separable convolution** â€” a pointwise mix in
channel space followed by a per-channel spatial filter.

The architectural hypothesis is that the cross-channel
correlations and the spatial correlations in feature maps are
sufficiently *decoupled* that they can â€” and should â€” be modeled
independently.  A regular convolution conflates the two; a
depthwise separable convolution separates them, dramatically
reducing both the parameter count and the FLOPs:



$$
D_K^2 \cdot M \cdot N \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
M \cdot N \;+\; D_K^2 \cdot M.
$$

Xception stacks 36 such layers, organised into 14 modules with
linear residual shortcuts around each module (similar to ResNet).
With essentially the *same* parameter budget as Inception-v3, it
outperforms it on ImageNet and substantially outperforms it on
the much larger JFT dataset.  The architecture is designed for a
$299 \times 299$ input â€” the same crop size as Inception-v3 â€”
so the spatial-reduction schedule matches that of its
predecessor exactly, isolating the depthwise-separable
factorisation as the sole source of accuracy improvement.
